title: The Art of Negotiation
summary: A narrator explains the key principles of effective negotiation, showing how preparation, listening, and creative thinking lead to better outcomes for everyone involved.
body: Negotiation is a skill that most people use every day without realising it. When you ask a colleague to swap shifts, discuss a price at a market, or persuade a friend to choose a different restaurant, you are negotiating. Yet many people feel uncomfortable when they enter formal negotiations, whether at work, in business, or when buying a car or a house. Understanding a few core principles can make a significant difference to how these conversations go. The first principle is preparation. Before you begin any negotiation, you should know what you want, why you want it, and what you are willing to accept if you cannot get everything. You should also think about what the other side wants and what might be important to them. People who prepare carefully are less likely to be surprised and more likely to remain calm. The second principle is listening. Many people approach a negotiation thinking mainly about their own position. They prepare arguments, rehearse their points, and wait for the other person to stop talking so that they can speak. This is a mistake. The most effective negotiators spend more time listening than speaking. When you truly listen, you often discover information that helps you. You might learn that the other person values speed more than price, or that they have a deadline you were unaware of. This kind of information allows you to make offers that cost you little but mean a great deal to the other side. The third principle is to look for options that benefit both parties. Many people treat negotiation as a competition where one person wins and the other loses. But in most situations, especially in long-term relationships, this approach is counterproductive. If the other person feels defeated, they may agree to your terms in the moment but resent you afterwards, which can damage the relationship and cause future problems. Skilled negotiators try to understand what the other person genuinely needs and find creative solutions that address those needs while also achieving their own goals. This approach is sometimes called a win-win solution. Of course, not every negotiation ends happily. Sometimes the two sides simply want different things and cannot bridge the gap. In those cases, it is important to know when to walk away. Agreeing to a bad deal just to avoid conflict is rarely a good strategy. The art of negotiation is not about being tough or clever — it is about being clear, honest, and genuinely interested in finding a solution that works.

annotations.exampleEn: She asked her colleague to swap shifts so she could attend the concert.
The training programme focused on the core principles of good customer service.
Good preparation made a significant difference to the outcome of the meeting.

annotations.meaningVi: luyện tập trước các luận điểm của mình
annotations.exampleEn: She rehearsed her key points in front of a mirror before the interview.

annotations.meaningVi: không biết về, không hay biết
annotations.exampleEn: He was unaware of the policy change until his manager told him.

annotations.phrase: make offers that cost you little
annotations.meaningVi: đưa ra đề nghị không tốn kém gì cho bạn
annotations.exampleEn: Flexibility on timing can be an offer that costs you little but helps the other side greatly.

annotations.meaningVi: có lợi cho cả hai bên
annotations.exampleEn: The new agreement was designed to benefit both parties equally.

annotations.meaningVi: phản tác dụng, gây hại hơn là có lợi
annotations.exampleEn: Shouting at employees is counterproductive — it lowers morale and reduces output.

annotations.meaningVi: oán giận, cảm thấy bất bình
annotations.exampleEn: She began to resent her manager for taking credit for her work.
Listen carefully to understand what the customer genuinely needs.

annotations.meaningVi: giải quyết những nhu cầu đó
annotations.exampleEn: The new policy was designed to address the needs of part-time workers.

annotations.meaningVi: giải pháp hai bên cùng có lợi
annotations.exampleEn: Flexible working hours turned out to be a win-win solution for both the employer and staff.

annotations.meaningVi: thu hẹp khoảng cách, tìm điểm chung
annotations.exampleEn: The two sides tried hard to bridge the gap between their positions.

annotations.meaningVi: rời bỏ cuộc đàm phán, không chấp nhận
annotations.exampleEn: If the price isn't right, be prepared to walk away from the deal.
Some people accept unfair terms just to avoid conflict.

annotations.meaningVi: các mối quan hệ lâu dài
annotations.exampleEn: Trust is essential in long-term relationships, whether personal or professional.

grammarNotes.title: Modal Verbs for Advice and Possibility — 'should', 'might', 'may'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài sử dụng nhiều động từ khiếm khuyết để diễn đạt lời khuyên và khả năng: 'you should know what you want', 'you should also think about', 'You might learn that...', 'they may agree to your terms'. 'Should' diễn đạt lời khuyên hoặc kỳ vọng. 'Might' và 'may' diễn đạt khả năng xảy ra nhưng không chắc chắn. Nắm vững sự khác biệt giúp bạn nói chính xác hơn về điều được khuyên và điều có thể xảy ra.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses modal verbs to express advice and possibility: 'you should know what you want', 'you should also think about', 'You might learn that...', 'they may agree to your terms'. 'Should' expresses advice or expectation. 'Might' and 'may' express possibility without certainty. Understanding the difference helps you speak more precisely about recommendations versus possibilities.

grammarNotes.title: Comparative Structures — 'more ... than', 'less likely to ... more likely to'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài dùng cấu trúc so sánh để đối chiếu các hành vi và kết quả: 'spend more time listening than speaking', 'less likely to be surprised and more likely to remain calm', 'values speed more than price'. Cấu trúc 'less/more likely to + infinitive' rất hữu ích khi nói về xác suất hoặc xu hướng. Đây là cách tự nhiên để so sánh thói quen, chiến lược và phẩm chất trong tiếng Anh.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses comparative structures to contrast behaviours and outcomes: 'spend more time listening than speaking', 'less likely to be surprised and more likely to remain calm', 'values speed more than price'. The structure 'less/more likely to + infinitive' is very useful when talking about probability or tendencies. This is a natural way to compare habits, strategies, and qualities in English.

translationVi:
Đàm phán là một kỹ năng mà hầu hết mọi người sử dụng hàng ngày mà không nhận ra. Khi bạn nhờ đồng nghiệp đổi ca, thảo luận về giá cả ở chợ, hoặc thuyết phục một người bạn chọn nhà hàng khác, bạn đang đàm phán. Tuy nhiên, nhiều người cảm thấy không thoải mái khi bước vào các cuộc đàm phán chính thức, dù là ở nơi làm việc, trong kinh doanh, hay khi mua xe hoặc nhà. Hiểu một vài nguyên tắc cốt lõi có thể tạo ra sự khác biệt đáng kể trong cách những cuộc trò chuyện này diễn ra.

Nguyên tắc đầu tiên là chuẩn bị. Trước khi bắt đầu bất kỳ cuộc đàm phán nào, bạn nên biết mình muốn gì, tại sao muốn, và điều mình sẵn sàng chấp nhận nếu không thể đạt được tất cả. Bạn cũng nên suy nghĩ về điều phía bên kia muốn và điều gì có thể quan trọng với họ. Những người chuẩn bị cẩn thận ít bị ngạc nhiên hơn và có nhiều khả năng giữ bình tĩnh hơn.

Nguyên tắc thứ hai là lắng nghe. Nhiều người tiếp cận cuộc đàm phán chủ yếu nghĩ về lập trường của mình. Họ chuẩn bị lập luận, luyện tập các điểm mấu chốt, và chờ người kia ngừng nói để họ có thể phát biểu. Đây là một sai lầm. Những người đàm phán hiệu quả nhất dành nhiều thời gian lắng nghe hơn là nói. Khi bạn thực sự lắng nghe, bạn thường khám phá ra thông tin giúp ích cho bạn. Bạn có thể học được rằng đối phương coi trọng tốc độ hơn giá cả, hoặc họ có một thời hạn mà bạn không biết. Loại thông tin này cho phép bạn đưa ra những đề nghị tốn kém ít của bạn nhưng có ý nghĩa lớn với đối phương.

Nguyên tắc thứ ba là tìm kiếm các lựa chọn có lợi cho cả hai bên. Nhiều người coi đàm phán như một cuộc cạnh tranh trong đó một người thắng và người kia thua. Nhưng trong hầu hết các tình huống, đặc biệt là trong các mối quan hệ lâu dài, cách tiếp cận này phản tác dụng. Nếu đối phương cảm thấy thất bại, họ có thể đồng ý với các điều kiện của bạn trong lúc đó nhưng oán giận bạn sau này, điều này có thể làm hỏng mối quan hệ và gây ra vấn đề trong tương lai. Những người đàm phán có kỹ năng cố gắng hiểu điều đối phương thực sự cần và tìm ra những giải pháp sáng tạo giải quyết những nhu cầu đó trong khi vẫn đạt được mục tiêu của họ. Cách tiếp cận này đôi khi được gọi là giải pháp hai bên cùng có lợi.

Tất nhiên, không phải cuộc đàm phán nào cũng kết thúc vui vẻ. Đôi khi hai bên chỉ đơn giản là muốn những thứ khác nhau và không thể thu hẹp khoảng cách. Trong những trường hợp đó, điều quan trọng là biết khi nào nên rời bỏ. Đồng ý với một thỏa thuận tồi chỉ để tránh xung đột hiếm khi là chiến lược tốt. Nghệ thuật đàm phán không phải là về việc cứng rắn hay khôn ngoan — mà là về việc rõ ràng, trung thực và thực sự quan tâm đến việc tìm ra giải pháp hiệu quả.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, when do most people use negotiation without realising it?
options: In everyday situations like swapping shifts or choosing a restaurant
When making large financial decisions like buying a house
questions.explanation: The passage gives examples of everyday negotiation: 'ask a colleague to swap shifts, discuss a price at a market, or persuade a friend to choose a different restaurant.'
questions.hint: The passage provides three common daily examples.

questions.prompt: What is the first principle of negotiation described in the passage?
questions.explanation: The passage states 'The first principle is preparation.'
questions.hint: The passage introduces three principles in order.

questions.prompt: What should you think about before starting a negotiation, according to the passage?
options: Only what you want and how to get it
What the other side is likely to say
What you want, why you want it, what you will accept, and what the other side wants
How long the negotiation is likely to take
questions.explanation: The passage says you should know 'what you want, why you want it, and what you are willing to accept' and also 'think about what the other side wants.'
questions.hint: The passage mentions your own goals and also the other side's perspective.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what mistake do many negotiators make?
options: They agree too quickly without asking for enough
They focus on their own position and do not truly listen
They become too emotional during discussions
They give away too much information early on
questions.explanation: The passage says many people 'approach a negotiation thinking mainly about their own position' and wait to speak rather than truly listening.
questions.hint: The passage says this approach is 'a mistake.'

questions.prompt: What is one example of useful information you might discover by listening carefully?
options: That the other person has more money than you thought
That the other person values speed more than price, or has a deadline
That the other person is willing to accept a very low offer
That the other person does not have authority to make the final decision
questions.explanation: The passage gives these examples: 'the other person values speed more than price, or that they have a deadline you were unaware of.'
questions.hint: The passage gives two specific examples of useful hidden information.

questions.prompt: What does the passage say about treating negotiation as a competition?
options: It is a good approach because it motivates you to do your best
It works well in business but not in personal relationships
It is counterproductive, especially in long-term relationships
It is necessary when the other side is not cooperating
questions.explanation: The passage says this approach 'is counterproductive' especially 'in long-term relationships.'
questions.hint: The passage uses a negative word to describe this competitive approach.

questions.prompt: What might happen if the other person feels defeated in a negotiation?
options: They will be impressed by your skill and offer more next time
They will likely walk away from all future negotiations
They may agree in the moment but resent you and damage the relationship
They will ask for a renegotiation immediately
questions.explanation: The passage says 'they may agree to your terms in the moment but resent you afterwards, which can damage the relationship.'
questions.hint: The passage distinguishes between the short-term agreement and the long-term consequences.

questions.prompt: What is a 'win-win solution' as described in the passage?
options: A solution where one side wins twice
A legal agreement that protects both parties
A creative solution that addresses both sides' genuine needs
A compromise where each side gives up exactly half
questions.explanation: The passage describes it as finding 'creative solutions that address those needs while also achieving their own goals.'
questions.hint: The passage explains win-win in terms of meeting both sides' genuine needs.

questions.prompt: What should you do when the two sides cannot bridge the gap?
options: Accept a bad deal to maintain the relationship
Continue negotiating indefinitely until an agreement is found
Ask a third party to decide the outcome
questions.explanation: The passage says 'it is important to know when to walk away.'
questions.hint: The passage uses a specific two-word phrase for this action.

questions.prompt: According to the final sentence, what is negotiation really about?
options: Being the toughest person in the room
Using clever tactics to gain an advantage
Being clear, honest, and genuinely interested in finding a solution
Getting the best possible deal regardless of the other side
questions.explanation: The passage concludes: 'it is about being clear, honest, and genuinely interested in finding a solution that works.'
questions.hint: The passage ends with a three-part definition of good negotiation.

questions.prompt: How does the passage characterise the best negotiators in terms of talking and listening?
options: They speak confidently and do not allow interruptions
They spend equal amounts of time speaking and listening
They spend more time listening than speaking
They say as little as possible to avoid giving away information
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'The most effective negotiators spend more time listening than speaking.'
questions.hint: The passage makes a direct comparison between two activities.

cloze.template: Negotiation is a skill that most people use every day without {{b1}} it. When you ask a colleague to {{b2}} shifts, discuss a price at a market, or persuade a friend to choose a different restaurant, you are negotiating. Understanding a few {{b3}} principles can make a significant difference to how these conversations go. Before you begin any negotiation, you should know what you want, why you want it, and what you are willing to {{b4}} if you cannot get everything. People who prepare carefully are less likely to be surprised and more likely to remain {{b5}}. The most effective negotiators spend more time listening than speaking. When you truly listen, you often discover information that helps you. You might learn that the other person values speed more than price, or that they have a {{b6}} you were unaware of. This kind of information allows you to make offers that cost you little but mean a great deal to the other side. Many people treat negotiation as a competition where one person wins and the other loses. But in most situations, especially in long-term relationships, this approach is {{b7}}. If the other person feels defeated, they may agree to your terms in the moment but {{b8}} you afterwards, which can damage the relationship and cause future problems. Skilled negotiators try to understand what the other person genuinely needs and find {{b9}} solutions that address those needs. This approach is sometimes called a {{b10}} solution. In those cases where sides cannot bridge the gap, it is important to know when to walk away. Agreeing to a bad deal just to avoid conflict is rarely a good strategy.

blanks.explanation: 'Without realising it' is the exact phrase — 'realising' means becoming aware of something.
'Swap shifts' is the set phrase — 'swap' means to exchange one thing for another informally.
'Core principles' is the collocation used — 'core' means the most essential or central part.
'Willing to accept' is the phrase — 'accept' means to agree to receive or agree to terms that are not perfect.
'More likely to remain calm' is the exact phrase — 'calm' describes a relaxed, unfrightened state under pressure.
'They have a deadline you were unaware of' — a deadline is a final date by which something must be done, often a key pressure point.
'Counterproductive' is the exact word used — it means producing the opposite of the desired result.
'Resent you afterwards' — 'resent' means to feel bitter or indignant about something done to you.
'Creative solutions' is the phrase — 'creative' means using imagination to find new or unexpected answers.
'Win-win solution' is the fixed term — it means an outcome that is beneficial to all parties involved.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The passage argues that listening is more important than speaking in negotiation, and that seeking a win-win outcome is better than trying to win at the other side's expense. Do you agree? Can you think of situations where a more competitive approach might actually be necessary or appropriate?
